question:
Problem B-4
Let \( n_{1}, n_{2}, \ldots, n_{s} \) be distinct integers such that
\[
\left(n_{1}+k\right)\left(n_{2}+k\right) \cdots\left(n_{5}+k\right)
\]
is an integral multiple of \( n_{1} n_{2} \cdots n_{s} \) for every integer \( k \). For each of the following assertions. give a proof or a counterexample:
(a) \( \left|n_{i}\right|=1 \) for some \( i \).
(b) If further all \( n \), are positive, then
\[
\left\{n_{1}, n_{2}, \ldots, n_{s}\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, s\} .
\]
solution:
B-4.
Let \( P_{k}=\left(n_{1}+k\right)\left(n_{2}+k\right) \cdots\left(n_{s}+k\right) \). We are given that \( P_{0} \mid P_{k} \) for all integers \( k \).
(a) \( P_{0} \mid P_{-1} \) and \( P_{0} \mid P_{1} \) together imply \( P_{0}^{2} \mid\left(P_{-1} P_{1}\right) \) or \( \left(n_{1}^{2} n_{2}^{2} \cdots n_{s}^{2}\right) \|\left(n_{1}^{2}-1\right)\left(n_{2}^{2}-1\right) \cdots \) \( \left.\left(n_{s}^{2}-1\right)\right] \).
No \( n_{1} \) can be zero since \( P_{k} \neq 0 \) for \( k \) sufficiently large. Thus, for each \( i, n_{i}^{2} \geqslant 1 \) and \( n_{i}^{2}>n_{t}^{2}-1 \) \( \geqslant 0 \). Hence \( P_{0}^{2}>P_{-1} P_{1} \geqslant 0 \). This and \( P_{0}^{2} \mid\left(P_{,} P_{1}\right) \) imply \( P_{-1} P_{1}=0 \). Then for some \( i,\left|n_{i}\right|=1 \).
(b) \( P_{k} \) is a polynomial in \( k \) of degree \( s \). Since \( P_{0} \) divides each \( P_{1}, P_{0} \) also divides the \( n \)th difference
\[
\sum_{i=0}^{s}(-1)^{\prime}\binom{s}{i} P_{t}=s!.
\]

Since \( P_{0}>0 \), this means that \( P_{0} \leqslant s! \). As \( P_{0} \) is a product of \( s \) distinct positive integers, it follows that
\[
\left\{n_{1}, n_{2}, \ldots, n_{s}\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, s\} .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-4
Let \( firstint, secondint, \ldots, lastint \) be distinct integers such that
\[
\left(firstint+shiftvar\right)\left(secondint+shiftvar\right) \cdots\left(n_{5}+shiftvar\right)
\]
is an integral multiple of \( firstint secondint \cdots lastint \) for every integer \( shiftvar \). For each of the following assertions. give a proof or a counterexample:
(a) \( \left|indexint\right|=1 \) for some \( indexvar \).
(b) If further all genericn , are positive, then
\[
\left\{firstint, secondint, \ldots, lastint\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, totals\} .
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
B-4.
Let \( polyshift=\left(firstint+shiftvar\right)\left(secondint+shiftvar\right) \cdots\left(lastint+shiftvar\right) \). We are given that \( polyzero \mid polyshift \) for all integers \( shiftvar \).
(a) \( polyzero \mid polynegone \) and \( polyzero \mid polyposone \) together imply \( polyzero^{2} \mid\left(polynegone\, polyposone\right) \) or \( \left(firstint^{2} secondint^{2} \cdots lastint^{2}\right) \|\left(firstint^{2}-1\right)\left(secondint^{2}-1\right) \cdots \left.\left(lastint^{2}-1\right)\right] \).
No \( firstint \) can be zero since \( polyshift \neq 0 \) for \( shiftvar \) sufficiently large. Thus, for each \( indexvar, indexint^{2} \geqslant 1 \) and \( indexint^{2}>tempint^{2}-1 \) \( \geqslant 0 \). Hence \( polyzero^{2}>polynegone\, polyposone \geqslant 0 \). This and \( polyzero^{2} \mid\left(polytemp\, polyposone\right) \) imply \( polynegone\, polyposone=0 \). Then for some \( indexvar,\left|indexint\right|=1 \).
(b) \( polyshift \) is a polynomial in \( shiftvar \) of degree \( totals \). Since \( polyzero \) divides each \( polyposone, polyzero \) also divides the genericn th difference
\[
\sum_{indexvar=0}^{totals}(-1)^{\prime}\binom{totals}{indexvar} polytemp=totals!.
\]

Since \( polyzero>0 \), this means that \( polyzero \leqslant totals! \). As \( polyzero \) is a product of \( totals \) distinct positive integers, it follows that
\[
\left\{firstint, secondint, \ldots, lastint\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, totals\} .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-4
Let \( quaysides, windchime, \ldots, afterglow \) be distinct integers such that
\[
\left(quaysides+marigolds\right)\left(windchime+marigolds\right) \cdots\left(n_{5}+marigolds\right)
\]
is an integral multiple of \( quaysides windchime \cdots afterglow \) for every integer \( marigolds \). For each of the following assertions. give a proof or a counterexample:
(a) \( \left|lachrymal\right|=1 \) for some \( hippocamp \).
(b) If further all \( foxgloves \), are positive, then
\[
\left\{quaysides, windchime, \ldots, afterglow\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, floodgate\} .
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-4.
Let \( brickwork=\left(quaysides+marigolds\right)\left(windchime+marigolds\right) \cdots\left(afterglow+marigolds\right) \). We are given that \( herdsmen \mid brickwork \) for all integers \( marigolds \).
(a) \( herdsmen \mid flannelry \) and \( herdsmen \mid snowfield \) together imply \( herdsmen^{2} \mid\left(flannelry snowfield\right) \) or \( \left(quaysides^{2} windchime^{2} \cdots afterglow^{2}\right) \|\left(quaysides^{2}-1\right)\left(windchime^{2}-1\right) \cdots \left.\left(afterglow^{2}-1\right)\right] \).
No \( quaysides \) can be zero since \( brickwork \neq 0 \) for \( marigolds \) sufficiently large. Thus, for each \( hippocamp, lachrymal^{2} \geqslant 1 \) and \( lachrymal^{2}>stringers^{2}-1 \geqslant 0 \). Hence \( herdsmen^{2}>flannelry snowfield \geqslant 0 \). This and \( herdsmen^{2} \mid\left(P_{,} snowfield\right) \) imply \( flannelry snowfield=0 \). Then for some \( hippocamp,\left|lachrymal\right|=1 \).
(b) \( brickwork \) is a polynomial in \( marigolds \) of degree \( floodgate \). Since \( herdsmen \) divides each \( snowfield, herdsmen \) also divides the \( foxgloves \)th difference
\[
\sum_{hippocamp=0}^{floodgate}(-1)^{\prime}\binom{floodgate}{hippocamp} mothballs = floodgate!.
\]

Since \( herdsmen>0 \), this means that \( herdsmen \leqslant floodgate! \). As \( herdsmen \) is a product of \( floodgate \) distinct positive integers, it follows that
\[
\left\{quaysides, windchime, \ldots, afterglow\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, floodgate\} .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem B-4
Let \( irrationalfirst, irrationalsecond, \ldots, irrationallast \) be distinct integers such that
\[
\left(irrationalfirst+constantval\right)\left(irrationalsecond+constantval\right) \cdots\left(n_{5}+constantval\right)
\]
is an integral multiple of \( irrationalfirst  irrationalsecond \cdots irrationallast \) for every integer \( constantval \). For each of the following assertions. give a proof or a counterexample:
(a) \( \left|irrationalaggregate\right|=1 \) for some \( aggregate \).
(b) If further all \( fractional \), are positive, then
\[
\left\{irrationalfirst, irrationalsecond, \ldots, irrationallast\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, infinitecount\} .
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-4.
Let \( quotientvar=\left(irrationalfirst+constantval\right)\left(irrationalsecond+constantval\right) \cdots\left(irrationallast+constantval\right) \). We are given that \( quotientzero \mid quotientvar \) for all integers \( constantval \).
(a) \( quotientzero \mid quotientminusone \) and \( quotientzero \mid quotientone \) together imply \( quotientzero^{2} \mid\left( quotientminusone quotientone \right) \) or \( \left(irrationalfirst^{2} irrationalsecond^{2} \cdots irrationallast^{2}\right) \|\left(irrationalfirst^{2}-1\right)\left(irrationalsecond^{2}-1\right) \cdots \) \( \left.\left(irrationallast^{2}-1\right)\right] \).
No \( irrationallast \) can be zero since \( quotientvar \neq 0 \) for \( constantval \) sufficiently large. Thus, for each \( aggregate, irrationalaggregate^{2} \geqslant 1 \) and \( irrationalaggregate^{2}>irrationaltotality^{2}-1 \) \( \geqslant 0 \). Hence \( quotientzero^{2}>quotientminusone quotientone \geqslant 0 \). This and \( quotientzero^{2} \mid\left(quotientminusone quotientone\right) \) imply \( quotientminusone quotientone=0 \). Then for some \( aggregate,\left|irrationalaggregate\right|=1 \).
(b) \( quotientvar \) is a polynomial in \( constantval \) of degree \( infinitecount \). Since \( quotientzero \) divides each \( quotientvar, quotientzero \) also divides the \( fractional \)th difference
\[
\sum_{aggregate=0}^{infinitecount}(-1)^{\prime}\binom{infinitecount}{aggregate} quotienttotality=infinitecount!.
\]
Since \( quotientzero>0 \), this means that \( quotientzero \leqslant infinitecount! \). As \( quotientzero \) is a product of \( infinitecount \) distinct positive integers, it follows that
\[
\left\{irrationalfirst, irrationalsecond, \ldots, irrationallast\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, infinitecount\} .
\]

garbled_string.question:
Problem B-4
Let \( xvkjlqne, ublgatjr, \ldots, fowcmnzd \) be distinct integers such that
\[
\left(xvkjlqne+bjsmfqre\right)\left(ublgatjr+bjsmfqre\right) \cdots\left(n_{5}+bjsmfqre\right)
\]
is an integral multiple of \( xvkjlqne\, ublgatjr \cdots fowcmnzd \) for every integer \( bjsmfqre \). For each of the following assertions, give a proof or a counterexample:
(a) \( \left|zsyrtmgh\right|=1 \) for some \( mxpdchqa \).
(b) If further all \( wqzldvke \) are positive, then
\[
\left\{xvkjlqne, ublgatjr, \ldots, fowcmnzd\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, ryvkqzds\} .
\]
garbled_string.solution:
B-4.
Let \( euyclnrb=\left(xvkjlqne+bjsmfqre\right)\left(ublgatjr+bjsmfqre\right) \cdots\left(fowcmnzd+bjsmfqre\right) \). We are given that \( qcfzktod \mid euyclnrb \) for all integers \( bjsmfqre \).
(a) \( qcfzktod \mid dlutwrsp \) and \( qcfzktod \mid jyhgzfbm \) together imply \( qcfzktod^{2} \mid\left(dlutwrsp\, jyhgzfbm\right) \) or \( \left(xvkjlqne^{2} ublgatjr^{2} \cdots fowcmnzd^{2}\right) \|\left(xvkjlqne^{2}-1\right)\left(ublgatjr^{2}-1\right) \cdots\left.\left(fowcmnzd^{2}-1\right)\right] \).
No \( xvkjlqne \) can be zero since \( euyclnrb \neq 0 \) for \( bjsmfqre \) sufficiently large. Thus, for each \( mxpdchqa, zsyrtmgh^{2} \geqslant 1 \) and \( zsyrtmgh^{2}>hdqspkei^{2}-1 \geqslant 0 \). Hence \( qcfzktod^{2}>dlutwrsp\, jyhgzfbm \geqslant 0 \). This and \( qcfzktod^{2} \mid\left(dlutwrsp\, jyhgzfbm\right) \) imply \( dlutwrsp\, jyhgzfbm=0 \). Then for some \( mxpdchqa,\left|zsyrtmgh\right|=1 \).
(b) \( euyclnrb \) is a polynomial in \( bjsmfqre \) of degree \( ryvkqzds \). Since \( qcfzktod \) divides each \( jyhgzfbm, qcfzktod \) also divides the \( wqzldvke \)th difference
\[
\sum_{mxpdchqa=0}^{ryvkqzds}(-1)^{\prime}\binom{ryvkqzds}{mxpdchqa} ovxinlma = ryvkqzds!.
\]
Since \( qcfzktod>0 \), this means that \( qcfzktod \leqslant ryvkqzds! \). As \( qcfzktod \) is a product of \( ryvkqzds \) distinct positive integers, it follows that
\[
\left\{xvkjlqne, ublgatjr, \ldots, fowcmnzd\right\}=\{1,2, \ldots, ryvkqzds\} .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let s be a positive integer and let m_{1},m_{2},\dots ,m_{s} be s distinct integers such that

\[\qquad  rac{(m_{1}+k)(m_{2}+k)\cdots (m_{s}+k)}{m_{1}m_{2}\cdots m_{s}}\in\mathbb Z \quad 	ext{for every } k\in\mathbb Z.\]

(a) Prove that |m_{j}|=1 for at least one index j.

(b) Show that if, in addition, all m_{j} are positive, then
\[\{m_{1},m_{2},\dots ,m_{s}\}=\{1,2,\dots ,s\}.\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Set P_{k}=\prod _{i=1}^{s}(m_{i}+k).  The hypothesis is exactly

  P_{0}\mid P_{k}  for every integer k.

Part (a).
Because P_{0}\mid P_{-1} and P_{0}\mid P_{1}, we have P_{0}^{2}\mid (P_{-1}P_{1}).  But
  P_{-1}P_{1}=\prod _{i=1}^{s}(m_{i}^{2}-1).
If |m_{i}|\geq 2 for every i, then m_{i}^{2}-1<m_{i}^{2}, so
  P_{-1}P_{1}<\prod _{i=1}^{s}m_{i}^{2}=P_{0}^{2}.
A nonzero multiple of P_{0}^{2} cannot be strictly less than P_{0}^{2}, so we reach a contradiction unless one factor m_{j}^{2}-1 is zero.  Hence |m_{j}|=1 for some j.

Part (b).
Now assume m_{1},\ldots ,m_{s} are positive.  Consider the polynomial P(k)=\prod _{i=1}^{s}(m_{i}+k), which is monic of degree s in k.  Its s-th forward difference with step -1 at k=2 is

  \Delta _{-1}^{s}P(2)=\sum _{j=0}^{s}(-1)^{j}C(s,j)P(2-j)

and by the standard finite-difference formula this equals s!(-1)^{s}.  Since P_{0} divides each term P(2-j), it divides the whole sum, so P_{0}\mid s!.  But P_{0}=m_{1}\cdots m_{s}>0, whence

  P_{0}\leq s!.

On the other hand, P_{0} is the product of s distinct positive integers.  The only way such a product can be \leq s! is if those integers are exactly 1,2,\ldots ,s.  Therefore

  {m_{1},m_{2},\ldots ,m_{s}}={1,2,\ldots ,s},

as required.

core_steps: Define P_k = ∏(n_i + k) so that the hypothesis reads P_0 | P_k for every integer k.
Part (a): Use k = -1 and k = 1 to get P_0^2 | P_{-1} P_1 = ∏(n_i^2 - 1).
Compare sizes: if every |n_i| ≥ 2 then P_{-1} P_1 < P_0^2, contradicting the divisibility, forcing some |n_i| = 1.
Part (b): Regard P_k as a degree-s polynomial; its s-th forward difference with step 1 is Δ^s P_0 = s!.
Because P_0 divides every P_k, it divides s!; but P_0 is the product of s distinct positive integers, so P_0 ≤ s!, whence the only possibility is {n_1,…,n_s} = {1,2,…,s}.

slot1.description: Base point of the finite-difference operator in Step 4 (they used 0, i.e. Δ^s P_0). Any integer starting point t would give the same value s! and keep the argument intact.

slot2.description: The step of the forward difference (they fixed it at 1). Using step h=±1 leaves Δ^s P equal to s!·h^s; since h^s=1 for h=±1, the reasoning (P_0 | s!) is unchanged.
